workflow_tags: data selection, meta regression, summary statistics, feature engineering, data standardization
domain_knowledge: 1. When working with multiple studies that are original and replicated, a preprocess & joining step is needed to gather the data required for metaregression analysis. 2. Combining such study data has to account for study & author names and may need multiple heuristics for the ideal join depending on the line of inquiry. 3. An extra step may also be required to handle duplicate values & missing pairs.

datasets.name: meta-regression_joined_data_heterogeneity_in_replication_projects.csv
datasets.description: Data created by merging data.csv and RProjects.csv

columns.study: Study identifier, usually names of authors from original study
columns.project.x: The replication project that the study was on (Psychology or Experimental Economics)
columns.ro: Effect estimate of original study on correlation scale
columns.rr: Effect estimate of replication study on correlation scale
columns.fiso: Effect estimate of original study transformed to Fisher-z scale
columns.fisr: Effect estimate of replication study transformed to Fisher-z scale
columns.se_fiso: Standard error of Fisher-z transformed effect estimate of original study
columns.se_fisr: Standard error of Fisher-z transformed effect estimate of replication study
columns.po: Two-sided p-value from significance test of effect estimate from original study
columns.pr: Two-sided p-value from significance test of effect estimate from replication study
columns.po1: One-sided p-value from significance test of effect estimate from original study (in the direction of the original effect estimate)
columns.pr1: One-sided p-value from significance test of effect estimate from replication study (in the direction of the original effect estimate)
columns.pm_belief: Peer belief about whether replication effect estimate will achieve statistical significance elicited through prediction market (only available for EERP and SSRP)
columns.pub_year: Year of Publication of the study
columns.journal: Journal in which the study was published
columns.discipline: Discipline of original paper. One of the following: Social, Cognitive or Economics
columns.length: Number of pages of original paper
columns.citations: Number of citations of original paper
columns.power.o: Post hoc power based on original effect size
columns.power.r: Post hoc power based on replication effect size
columns.power_planned.r: Planned power of the replication based on planned N and original ES
columns.experiment_country.o: Country where original experiment was conducted
columns.experiment_country.r: Country where replication is to be conducted
columns.experiment_language.o: Language used with subjects in original experiment (English, German, Dutch, Polish, Hebrew, French, Italian, Arabic, Spanish, Korean)
columns.experiment_language.r: Language to be used with subjects in replication (English, Polish, German, Dutch, Italian, Portuguese, Malay, Turkish, Czech, Arabic, Spanish)
columns.online.o: If the original experiment was conducted online (1: yes, 0: no)
columns.online.r: If the replication was conducted online (1: yes, 0: no)
columns.compensation.o: Compensation in original experiment (credit, cash, nothing, mixed)
columns.compensation.r: Compensation in replication (credit, cash, nothing, mixed)
columns.subjects.o: Type of subjects used in original experiment (students, online, anyone, community)
columns.subjects.r: Type of subjects used in replication (students, online, anyone, community)
columns.endprice: Final market price in prediction market
columns.transactions: Number of transactions in prediction market
columns.trading_volume: Total volume of traded stocks in prediction market
columns.replicated: Binary outcome variable; study is replicated if p <= 0.05 and effect goes in the same direction as the original
columns.project.y: The replication project that the study was on (rpp: Psychology or ee: Experimental Economics)
columns.relative_es: The continuous outcome variable; the standardized replication effect size to the original effect | relative effect size = (replication effect size / original effect size)
columns.n_authors.o: Number of authors in original study
columns.author_citations_avg.o: Average number of citations of authors in original study
columns.author_citations_max.o: Number of citations of the author in original study with the highest citation count
columns.authors_male.o: Ratio of male authors in original study
columns.seniority.o: Most senior author in the original paper (Professor, Associate Professor, Assistant, Researcher, Assistant Professor)
columns.author_citations_avg.r: Average number of citations of authors in replication study
columns.author_citations_max.r: Number of citations of the author in original study with the highest citation count
columns.authors_male.r: Ratio of male authors in replication
columns.seniority.r: Most senior author in the original paper (Professor, Associate Professor, Assistant, Researcher, Assistant Professor)
columns.lab_id: Unique id for each replication lab
columns.es_80power: Standardized effect size required in replication to achieve 80% power
columns.same_country: Original study and replication are in the same country
columns.same_language: Original study and replication are in the same language
columns.same_online: Original study and replication are both conducted online
columns.same_subjects: Original study and replication use same type of subjects
columns.us_lab.o: Original experiment lab in the US
columns.us_lab.r: Replication experiment lab in the US

queries.question: For which domains do the effect size estimates tend to be larger in original studies compared to replication studies?
In Experimental Economics, what is the average effect estimate in original studies as compared to that in replication studies?
In Psychology, what is the average effect estimate in original studies as compared to that in replication studies?
Which factor in Experimental Economics has a value of 0.57 on the Fisher-z scale in original studies compared to 0.31 in replication studies?
